feedback.text: - Considering Ubisoft's infamous quote about "players needing to get used to not owning their games"; - Considering that Activision-Blizzard killed a game ("Overwatch"), of which they sold over 50 million copies, which made them billions of dollars they kept without consequences; - Considering hundreds of video games that were sold in the past, even recently, which are no longer playable today as documented by the "Stop Killing Games Wiki" in its "Dead game list" page, which is publicly available online; - Considering other hundreds of video games that were sold in the past, even recently, for which there is currently no end-of-life plan as documented by the "Stop Killing Games Wiki" in its "Dead game list" page, which is publicly available online; - Given the push of the video game industry to increasingly require, even without any apparent practical motivation, that consumers are always connected online to use a product (the video game) which is not subscription-based and for which they have already paid in full; - Considering that, even if the video game was distributed free of charge, it could contain in-app purchases, which link the consumer's ability to access and use the product thus purchased to the survival and the playability of the video game itself; - Considering the imbalance of power, that the video game industry has been tilting in its favor for years to the detriment of consumers, given by the exclusive and arbitrary control over access to and use of software or content; - Given the lack of regulatory clarity about defining if video games (and software in general) sold online are a product or a service, which allows the video game industry to sell consumers something that is passed off as a product, especially through the language used at the time of sale, but which is often and willingly treated and managed as a service once sold, to the detriment of consumers once again; - Considering some of the issues already addressed by the "Digital Fairness Act", that can already be found in video games now, such as "dark patterns in online interfaces", "addictive design of digital services that pushes users to [...] spend more money, such as, gambling-like features in video games" and "difficulties with managing digital subscriptions"; - Considering the recent European Citizens' Initiative named "Stop Destroying Videogames", which has almost reached the support of one and a half million signatures at the time of writing; I personally would like to kindly ask, for all the reasons listed above, that the "Digital Fairness Act" would also include clauses, rules or measures that protect the ability to access and use video games already purchased by consumers, who must retain the right to use in some way the software for which they have already paid. The issue I would like to be discussed and addressed by the "Digital Fairness Act" is the following: "Publishers permanently disabling video games they have already sold. Some publishers are selling video games under terms that may already violate Directive 93/13/EEC and deliberately prevent customers from retaining or repairing their purchase." Thank you for your attention to this matter.
